nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, tributaries, oxbows, and floodplain lakes; shaded areas with leaf litter, driftwood, and overhanging vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting with Echinodorus, Vallisneria, and floating plants

diet.notes: The Spotted Headstander is omnivorous with a lean toward plant matter and small invertebrates. In the wild, it feeds on insects, larvae, terrestrial fruits, seeds, and algae, and has been reported to consume fish scales on occasion. In the aquarium, sinking algae wafers and spirulina-based foods make good staples, since the fish's characteristic head-down posture means it rarely feeds at the surface. Supplement with live or frozen Daphnia, Artemia, Moina, and bloodworm, and offer blanched vegetables such as spinach, zucchini, or cucumber and small pieces of fruit from time to time.

goodWith: small to medium tetras (e.g., rummy nose, cardinal, neon)
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving species that outcompete for food
Anostomus anostomus (Striped Headstander) — that species is aggressive and a plant-eater; the two are often confused but require very different tankmates

imageCandidates.notes: Clear lateral profile showing characteristic head-down posture and spotted pattern; attributed to Leandro Sousa.
Rio Negro specimen; shows natural blackwater coloration.
Essequibo (Guyana) specimen; useful for geographic comparison.

summary: The Spotted Headstander is a peaceful, mid-sized South American characin best known for the distinctive 45-degree head-down angle it holds while resting and swimming. Unlike the similarly-named Striped Headstander, this species is genuinely peaceful and plant-safe. It does best in soft, acidic blackwater conditions in a biologically mature aquarium.
careNotes:
The Spotted Headstander, known scientifically as Chilodus punctatus, belongs to the family Chilodontidae. It shares its head-down resting and swimming posture — held at roughly 45 degrees — with a different group of fish called headstanders in family Anostomidae, but the two groups are not closely related and are not a match in temperament or care. The Striped Headstander (Anostomus anostomus) in particular is often confused with this species due to the shared posture; however, it is aggressive, a confirmed plant-eater, and incompatible with many community fish, while the Spotted Headstander is generally considered peaceful and plant-safe.

Keep in a biologically mature aquarium of at least 81 L with a 90 cm footprint, filtered to turn over the tank volume 4–5 times per hour. Soft, acidic water is important: target pH 5.5–7.0, gH 1–8, temperature 22–26°C for long-term health. Tannin-stained water (achieved with Indian almond or catappa leaves, or driftwood) is highly beneficial and reflects the blackwater biotopes of the rio Negro and Essequibo — the rivers from which most specimens originate.

Maintain in groups of five or more. Smaller groups tend to produce shyness and low-level squabbling among individuals. Provided the tank has sufficient cover — dense planting, driftwood, leaf litter, and subdued lighting — any intraspecific spats rarely result in physical damage. A dark substrate and low ambient light generally bring out the best coloration and reduce stress.

Because the characteristic head-down feeding angle means the fish rarely takes food at the surface, sinking foods are essential: offer algae wafers, spirulina pellets, and catappa-leaf biofilm as staples, supplemented with frozen Daphnia, Artemia, and Moina two or three times per week. Blanched vegetables are also accepted.

New specimens may take a few days to settle and begin feeding; dimming the tank and offering live Daphnia can encourage reluctant individuals. The species is sensitive to dissolved organic waste, so pristine water quality and regular partial water changes are essential. See also: [striped-headstander] (Anostomus anostomus) — superficially similar posture but completely different family, temperament, and care requirements.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely documented. Presumably an egg-scatterer with no parental care, likely triggered by simulating the onset of the wet season — increasing soft, warm, acidic water and offering live foods. Specific protocols are not established in hobbyist literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Chilodus punctatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Chilodontidae, Characiformes), native range (Ecuador, Peru, Brazil, Colombia, Guyana, Suriname), water parameters (temp 20–28°C, pH 5.0–7.5, hardness 18–179 ppm ≈ gH 1–10), adult size (70–80 mm SL ≈ 8–10 cm TL), minimum tank (81 L, 90×30 cm base), diet (omnivore — insects, larvae, fruits, algae, vegetables, live/frozen), temperament (peaceful), grouping and conspecific aggression notes, biotope (blackwater with driftwood, leaf litter), and a note that captive breeding has occurred but details are unavailable.

additional.name: FishBase — Chilodus punctatus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Chilodontidae, Characiformes), native range countries (Brazil/Pará/Apeú, Guyana, Suriname, Venezuela/western Orinoco), water parameters (temp 24–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness up to 10 dH), trophic level 2.8 (omnivore). FishBase max TL of 18 cm appears to be an outlier or data entry error inconsistent with SeriouslyFish (70–80 mm SL) and hobbyist consensus of 8–10 cm; 10 cm used as maximum.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Chilodus punctatus
additional.notes: Confirmed 45° head-down swimming posture, adult size up to 7 cm, distribution (northeastern South America, Loreto/Peru), IUCN Least Concern (2023), nocturnal/shade-dwelling habits, Chilodontidae family placement. Described body coloration (grayish-green with rows of brown specks, black longitudinal band, silver throat/belly).

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity; no reliable protocol is established. Presumably an egg-scatterer with no parental care. Simulating wet-season conditions (soft acidic water, increased temperature, heavy live-food feeding) is the recommended approach. Confirmed to have occurred in captivity but details are unpublished.
